LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source download from the project's official GitHub repository using a tagged release tarball. The `sha256sums` array contains a valid checksum, indicating that the source integrity is verified after download. The global scope of the PKGBUILD contains no command substitution, external code execution, or arbitrary shell invocations. The `pkgver` function is not defined, so no dynamic version fetching occurs.

The commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only source the PKGBUILD, verify the source URL, download the tarball, and extract it if necessary. None of the potentially unsafe functions (`prepare`, `build`, `check`, `package`) are executed due to the flags used. There is no evidence of malicious code execution, exfiltration, or unsafe behavior during the sourcing phase.

All defined sources and operations are consistent with standard, safe AUR packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious behavior during source fetch and parse
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices for a Rust-based project. It fetches the source from the project's legitimate GitHub repository using a tagged release URL, and the corresponding sha256 checksum is provided and matches the expected value. The build process sets appropriate environment variables (RUSTUP_TOOLCHAIN, CARGO_TARGET_DIR) and uses standard cargo commands (cargo update, cargo fetch, cargo build) in a predictable and non-malicious manner. The use of `cargo update` in prepare() is notable, as it modifies the dependency tree, but since the source is a fixed release tag and the build uses `--frozen` later, this is likely intended to ensure consistency between Cargo.lock and Cargo.toml and does not constitute a supply chain risk.

The check() function performs a basic smoke test by compiling and running a small TypeScript program with the built binary. While it uses /tmp for temporary files, this is harmless and does not involve exfiltration or remote execution. No network access, file manipulation outside the build directory, or execution of third-party scripts occurs. All installed files (binary, static libraries, license) are directly related to the package's declared functionality.

The package installs into standard system paths under $pkgdir, and the license and dependencies are properly declared. The operations in package() are minimal and appropriate. There is no obfuscated code, dangerous commands, or suspicious activity. All actions are consistent with building and packaging a legitimate native compiler.
</details>
<summary>
Standard AUR packaging, no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for install.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `install.sh` script is designed to download and install the Perry tool from its official GitHub repository (PerryTS/perry). It performs standard operations for an installer: detecting the OS and architecture, locating the latest release with a matching pre-built binary, downloading the appropriate tarball, extracting it, and installing the binary and any associated static libraries to standard system locations (`/usr/local/bin` and `/usr/local/lib`).

The script uses conservative practices: it avoids executing any downloaded code directly, instead relying on `curl` to fetch a signed release asset from GitHub’s trusted domain. It validates the existence of the required artifact by probing release URLs with HEAD requests before downloading. Temporary files are cleaned up via a trap, and the installation handles permissions gracefully by falling back to `sudo` only when necessary. All network operations are scoped to the declared GitHub repository, and no obfuscated commands, unexpected network calls, or data exfiltration mechanisms are present.

While the script encourages piping from `curl`, which is inherently risky if the source is untrusted, the content itself does not contain malicious logic. It follows common installer patterns without deviating into supply-chain attack behaviors such as downloading from third-party hosts, executing remote scripts, or tampering with unrelated system files.
</details>
<summary>
Installer fetches and installs from trusted GitHub repo; no malicious behavior detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for index.ts:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided TypeScript file `index.ts` is a straightforward adapter that bridges Hono's fetch handler interface with Perry's Fastify-based server runtime. It creates a Fastify server instance, sets up a catch-all route to convert Fastify's request into a Web `Request`, invokes the Hono app's fetch handler, and translates the resulting `Response` back into a Fastify reply. The code performs only local request/response transformation and HTTP server binding—there are no network requests to external domains, no use of `eval` or shell commands, no file system manipulations, and no data exfiltration.

All dependencies are imported from standard packages (`fastify`), and the logic is confined to request handling and header/body mapping. The comments indicate clear intent and document version requirements (e.g., Perry ≥ 0.5.1027) related to API correctness, not security bypasses. The use of `JSON.stringify` and `res.text()` is standard for response handling and consistent with Hono's expected behavior. No obfuscation, dynamic code evaluation, or unsafe practices are present.

This file serves as a legitimate compatibility layer and follows expected patterns for runtime adapters. It does not introduce supply-chain risks or malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code; standard Hono-Perry adapter
</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `build.rs` file is a standard Rust build script used to configure compilation of C code via the `cc` crate. It compiles a vendored C implementation (`miniaudio_impl.c`) and sets appropriate compiler flags to suppress warnings from the miniaudio library. The script conditionally links system libraries or frameworks depending on the target platform (Linux, Android, Windows, or Apple), which is normal and expected behavior for a cross-platform audio library.

All source files are locally vendored under the `vendor/` directory, and no network requests, dynamic code evaluation, or external downloads occur. The script uses standard Cargo features such as `cargo:rerun-if-changed` and `cargo:rustc-link-lib` to control the build process. The comments clarify the rationale for linking on Apple platforms — for build hygiene on macOS, even though the final binary uses a different audio backend.

There is no obfuscation, no execution of external scripts, and no suspicious or potentially malicious behavior. The code aligns with standard practices in the Rust ecosystem for integrating C libraries with conditional compilation and linking.
</details>
<summary>
Standard build script with no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for cli.ts:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `cli.ts` file is a TypeScript script intended as a CLI entry point for a tool named `perry-styling`. It reads a JSON file containing design tokens, processes them via imported functions (`parseTokens`, `generateTheme`), and writes a generated TypeScript file (`theme.ts`) to a specified output path. The script parses command-line arguments to support the `generate` command with `--tokens` and `--out` options, validates inputs, and handles basic I/O errors.

The code operates entirely offline and performs only local file system operations: reading an input JSON file and writing a generated TypeScript file. It does not make network requests, execute external commands, spawn shells, or use dangerous constructs like `eval`, `child_process`, or `curl`/`wget`. The file does not import or execute any code dynamically from remote sources. Comments mentioning `perry compile src/cli.ts -o dist/cli` suggest a build or bundling step, but no such operation is performed within the script itself.

All operations are consistent with a design-token code generation tool. There is no obfuscated code, suspicious encoding, or evidence of data exfiltration. The script only accesses files specified by the user via CLI arguments and does not access sensitive system files, environment variables, or user data beyond what is necessary for its declared purpose.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious behavior; standard CLI tool for code generation
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for perry.js:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `perry.js` file is a Node.js wrapper script designed to resolve and execute a platform-specific binary based on the current OS, architecture, and libc type (glibc vs musl). It determines the appropriate `@perryts/perry-*` package by checking `process.platform`, `process.arch`, and using `isMusl()` to detect musl-based Linux systems (e.g., Alpine). The script then attempts to load the corresponding prebuilt binary via `require.resolve` and spawns it with inherited stdio and passed-through command-line arguments.

All operations are confined to resolving and executing expected, platform-matched binaries from packages under the `@perryts` scope, with clear error handling for unsupported platforms or missing dependencies. The script does not download or execute code from external sources at runtime, nor does it exfiltrate system information. It uses standard Node.js APIs (`child_process.spawn`, `process.report`, `fs.readFileSync`) for legitimate platform detection and process control, consistent with common native binary distribution patterns in npm (e.g., via `node-bindings` or `prebuild`).

The only network-related behavior is a static error message directing users to file an issue at `https://github.com/PerryTS/perry/issues`—this is not an active telemetry or data exfiltration mechanism. There is no obfuscated code, suspicious base64 usage, or unauthorized system access. The behavior aligns with expected packaging practices for cross-platform Node.js tools.
</details>
<summary>
Standard platform-specific binary wrapper; no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for perry.rb:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `perry.rb` file is a Homebrew formula, not an Arch AUR PKGBUILD, despite being in a package named "perry". However, treating it as untrusted input per instructions, the analysis proceeds. The formula fetches precompiled binaries for macOS from the project's official GitHub releases and source code for Linux, where it builds using `cargo`. All sources are from the legitimate upstream repository (https://github.com/PerryTS/perry), and the use of `cargo build` on Linux is standard for Rust-based projects. The `sha256` hashes are marked as "PLACEHOLDER", which would be a hygiene concern in practice—indicating checksums are not pinned—but this is not inherently malicious, especially during development or templating. No network requests, file operations, or system modifications go beyond what is expected for a package manager recipe. There is no obfuscated code, data exfiltration, or execution of downloaded scripts.

The `caveats` section provides legitimate user guidance about linker dependencies and usage examples, with no harmful commands. The test block verifies basic functionality in isolation and does not access external resources. All operations are transparent, scoped to the package, and consistent with standard packaging practices. The use of `bin.install` and `lib.install` within the `install` method is normal for Homebrew formulas. While placeholders for checksums are not ideal security hygiene, they do not elevate this to an unsafe rating because the sources are still from the declared upstream and no malicious behavior is present.

Notably, the formula does not employ dangerous patterns such as `curl | bash`, `eval`, or runtime downloads of executable code. It does not modify system files outside the package installation directory, nor does it attempt to exfiltrate environment data, SSH keys, or other sensitive information. The build process on Linux uses standard Rust tooling, and no unusual dependencies or post-install network calls are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard Homebrew formula with placeholder checksums; no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for parity_status.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `parity_status.sh` is a Bash utility designed to discover and run test parity checks for the Perry project against Node.js's `--experimental-strip-types` behavior. It collects directories under `test-parity/node-suite/`, checks for the presence of TypeScript (`.ts`) files, and delegates execution to `run_module_parity.sh` with the discovered module list. If modules are provided as arguments, it skips discovery and uses them directly.

The script demonstrates no malicious behavior: it operates only on local files, makes no network requests, avoids dangerous commands like `curl` or `eval`, and does not exfiltrate data. It uses standard POSIX and Bash features (e.g., `compgen`, `read`, `printf`) for file discovery, consistent with testing workflows. The `exec` call to `run_module_parity.sh` is a standard way to chain scripts in test tooling and does not involve code generation or remote execution.

While the script lacks explicit input validation on directory names, it only processes local paths under a fixed directory and is intended for developer use in a controlled environment. No signs of obfuscation, unauthorized system access, or supply-chain compromise are present.
</details>
<summary>
Local test script with no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for wasm_runtime.js:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The file contains a dangerous use of `eval` on user-controlled content within an iframe, which creates a critical security vulnerability. In the `ui_eval_js` function (lines 1075-1088), the code retrieves a string `js` passed from WASM and executes it using `eval` in the context of an iframe's contentWindow:

```javascript
const result = el.contentWindow?.eval(js);
```

This allows arbitrary JavaScript code execution in the context of the iframe, which could lead to XSS attacks, data exfiltration, or worse. The function processes untrusted input from the WASM layer (which may originate from potentially malicious sources) and executes it with no sanitization or protection mechanisms.

Additionally, the file contains a base64 decoding mechanism for WASM binaries (`getWasmBinary` function) that relies on content stored in `window.__perryWasmB64`. While base64 decoding itself is not inherently malicious, when combined with the `eval` vulnerability, it creates an attack vector where an attacker could inject malicious WASM code that then triggers harmful JavaScript execution via the `ui_eval_js` function.

These vulnerabilities represent supply chain risks as they could be exploited to execute arbitrary code in the browser context, bypass same-origin policies through iframe manipulation, and potentially compromise user data or system integrity.
</details>
<summary>Arbitrary code execution via eval in iframe context</summary>
</security_assessment>

<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided JavaScript file, `web_runtime.js`, is a comprehensive frontend runtime for the Perry UI framework, mapping high-level UI abstractions to DOM elements in a web environment. It implements standard UI functionality including widget creation (e.g., buttons, text fields, stacks), state management with reactivity, styling, animations, and access to browser APIs such as localStorage, Web Audio, clipboard, and media.

The code shows no signs of malicious behavior. It does not make unauthorized network requests, exfiltrate data, execute dynamic code (e.g., via eval, Function, or base64 decoding), or interact with unexpected external services. All network-related operations (such as file downloads during audio recording) are user-initiated and occur client-side via standard Web APIs (e.g., `URL.createObjectURL`, `a.click()`). The use of `localStorage` is limited to application state preferences and app group data, consistent with expected web app behavior.

Notably, the file includes pre-populated demo content in a virtual file system (`__perryFileCache`), which serves as a tutorial or starter project for users of the Hone IDE. This data is self-contained and does not leak to remote servers. Other APIs like `perry_system_open_url` use `window.open`, and audio capture requires explicit user permission via `getUserMedia`. All exposed global functions are related to UI, state, or platform abstraction and do not introduce backdoors or remote execution capabilities.

While the file is large and complex, every component aligns with the legitimate purpose of providing a rich, native-like UI runtime for web deployment of Perry applications.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code; standard UI runtime for web
</summary>
</security_assessment>

All commands and file operations are scoped to local testing and temporary output directories (e.g., `/tmp/llvm_sweep_out`).

There are no signs of malicious behavior. The script does not make network requests, exfiltrate data, download or execute remote code, or modify system files. The use of `bc`, `echo`, and process substitution is for arithmetic and output comparison, not obfuscation. While `cargo build` is executed, it is part of normal Rust project development and targets the local crate, not an external dependency injection.

The script follows safe and transparent practices, focusing solely on regression testing of the `perry` compiler. Despite using environment variables like `PERRY_TIMEOUT` and `PERRY_SWEEP_DIR`, these are used for configuration, not covert channels. No obfuscated code, suspicious strings, or unexpected executions are present.
</details>

</security_assessment>

# Issues (1 total)

## wasm_runtime.js

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: Arbitrary code execution via eval in iframe context

Details:

The file contains a dangerous use of `eval` on user-controlled content within an iframe, which creates a critical security vulnerability. In the `ui_eval_js` function (lines 1075-1088), the code retrieves a string `js` passed from WASM and executes it using `eval` in the context of an iframe's contentWindow:

```javascript
const result = el.contentWindow?.eval(js);
```

This allows arbitrary JavaScript code execution in the context of the iframe, which could lead to XSS attacks, data exfiltration, or worse. The function processes untrusted input from the WASM layer (which may originate from potentially malicious sources) and executes it with no sanitization or protection mechanisms.

Additionally, the file contains a base64 decoding mechanism for WASM binaries (`getWasmBinary` function) that relies on content stored in `window.__perryWasmB64`. While base64 decoding itself is not inherently malicious, when combined with the `eval` vulnerability, it creates an attack vector where an attacker could inject malicious WASM code that then triggers harmful JavaScript execution via the `ui_eval_js` function.

These vulnerabilities represent supply chain risks as they could be exploited to execute arbitrary code in the browser context, bypass same-origin policies through iframe manipulation, and potentially compromise user data or system integrity.

---

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This file is a standard Node.js wrapper that resolves a platform-specific binary from an optional-dependency package (@perryts/perry-*) and spawns it. The code:
- Maps host OS/arch to a package name
- Detects musl libc by reading `/etc/os-release` (normal for platform detection)
- Uses `require.resolve` to locate the binary (no dynamic code execution)
- Spawns the binary with `child_process.spawn` and passes CLI arguments through
- Handles signal forwarding and exit codes

There is no obfuscation, no network fetches, no unexpected file writes, and no execution of untrusted content. The binary it runs is installed by npm as a declared dependency, not downloaded on the fly. This is a safe, conventional packaging pattern for distributing native binaries via npm.
</details>
<summary>Standard platform-specific binary wrapper; no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

No network requests or downloads occur; the only external command is the local `run_parity_tests.sh`. There is no obfuscated code, no use of `eval`, `curl`, `wget`, `base64`, or any file operations outside of reading its own test output. <decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `web_runtime.js` is the web runtime for the Perry UI framework. It implements widget creation, state management, canvas operations, audio capture, file system stubs, and other standard UI runtime features. All network accesses (`perry_system_open_url`, `perry_ui_image_create_url`) are expected for a UI framework and use standard browser APIs. Audio capture uses `getUserMedia` with user consent. Recording triggers a standard browser download. The file system functions (`fs_readFileSync`, etc.) operate on an in-memory cache (`__perryFileCache`) that is pre-populated with demo project content, not the real filesystem. There is no exfiltration of data, no download and execution of remote code, no obfuscated or encoded payloads, and no backdoors. The code is well-structured and consistent with legitimate packaging.
</details>
<summary>Standard web runtime for Perry UI framework; no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for wasm_runtime.js:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a JavaScript runtime bridge for a WASM-based UI framework (Perry). It implements NaN-boxing, string tables, handle stores, and a large set of functions for object manipulation, UI widget dispatch, audio, and other browser APIs. The suspicious pattern `contentWindow?.eval(js)` appears in a comment line and is likely part of a function that executes JavaScript inside an iframe, which is a legitimate feature for a UI framework that embeds web content. The comment notes "Same-origin only", limiting the risk. There is no evidence of obfuscated code, exfiltration, network requests to unexpected hosts, or backdoor installation. All other code consists of standard WASM runtime operations and upstream application functionality for the Perry framework. No malicious injection or supply-chain attack indicators are present.
</details>
<summary>
Legitimate WASM runtime; no malicious behavior detected.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for run_llvm_sweep.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This shell script is a test harness for the Perry package's LLVM backend. It compiles TypeScript test files, runs the resulting binaries with a timeout, compares the output to Node.js, and reports MATCH/DIFF/CRASH/COMPILE_FAIL counts. The script performs only local operations: building the project with `cargo build --release`, compiling test files with `$PERRY compile`, running the binary, and comparing output to Node.js. There are no network requests (except the implicit `cargo build` fetching dependencies, which is standard package build behavior). No obfuscated code, no suspicious commands like `eval` or `base64`, no exfiltration of data. The script is a straightforward testing tool for the package's own functionality, consistent with ordinary packaging practices.
</details>
<summary>Standard test harness for LLVM backend, no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

/tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-b1zpz0ah/perry-0.5.1182.tar.gz: [SKIPPED] Skipping binary file: perry-0.5.1182.tar.gz

data.reasoning: The PKGBUILD is a standard Rust package build: it fetches a pinned GitHub release tarball with a valid SHA256 checksum, runs cargo update/fetch/build, and a local smoke test in check(). None of the files flagged execute during makepkg build/install. The qwen 'unsafe' verdict is based on a `contentWindow?.eval(js)` call inside wasm_runtime.js, a browser-side UI runtime that is application source code shipped in the upstream repo — it is NOT executed during package build/install and is not referenced by any PKGBUILD function or .install hook. An eval inside a same-origin iframe for a UI framework's eval-JS feature is application functionality, not a supply-chain/build-time threat against the user installing the package. This is a false positive in the AUR audit context.
data.disagreement_analysis: qwen3 flagged wasm_runtime.js as unsafe due to `el.contentWindow?.eval(js)` in a ui_eval_js function. This is a runtime feature of the Perry UI/WASM framework (an intentional 'evaluate JS in iframe' capability), not malicious injection and not executed during package installation. deepseek correctly contextualized this as legitimate same-origin iframe functionality. The disagreement stems from qwen applying a web-application XSS/vulnerability lens rather than an AUR build-time threat-model lens.
coverage_issues: Neither model could inspect the contents of perry-0.5.1182.tar.gz beyond extracted source (binary skip is expected); the actual prebuilt binary distributed via npm/homebrew installers was not analyzable, but those installers are not invoked by the PKGBUILD.

Both models reviewed essentially the same high-priority set; qwen added index.ts and cli.ts/parity_status.sh while deepseek added run_tests/run_module_parity/run_parity_tests — minor divergence, no critical file uniquely missed.
false_positives: qwen3's UNSAFE on wasm_runtime.js: `contentWindow?.eval(js)` is an intentional same-origin iframe JS-eval feature of the UI framework, application runtime code not executed during makepkg build/install — out of scope for AUR build-time threat model.
Base64 WASM decoding (getWasmBinary) flagged as contributing risk — base64-embedded WASM is a normal browser packaging technique, not malicious.
false_negatives: None of significance for the build-time threat model. install.sh and perry.rb use unverified curl downloads (no checksum/GPG), correctly noted as hygiene-only; these are not invoked by the PKGBUILD so pose no build-time risk.
learnings: Reinforce in the prompt that findings in application/runtime source (browser JS, WASM runtimes, UI framework code) are only relevant if executed during PKGBUILD prepare/build/check/package or .install hooks; eval/XSS-style findings in shipped app code are out of scope unless build-invoked.
Add explicit guidance distinguishing 'vulnerability in the software' (web-app security) from 'supply-chain threat to the user running makepkg' — qwen conflated the two.
When a model flags eval/exec, require it to trace whether the code path is reachable from the PKGBUILD build/install chain before assigning UNSAFE.
Models should consistently note that auxiliary installers (install.sh, homebrew .rb) not referenced by the PKGBUILD are informational-only; both did this correctly here.
